author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
Why God Makes of Some Sheep, Others Not.
title: Why God Makes of Some Sheep, Others Not.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But wherefore does God make these men sheep, and those not, since with Him there is no acceptance of persons?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: This is the very question which the blessed apostle thus answers to those who propose it with more curiosity than propriety, “O man, who art thou that repliest against God?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Does the thing formed say to him that formed it, Wherefore hast thou made me thus?”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: This is the very question which belongs to that depth desiring to look into which the same apostle was in a certain measure terrified, and exclaimed, “Oh the depth of the riches of the wisdom and the knowledge of God! how unsearchable are His judgments, and His ways past finding out!

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For who has known the mind of the Lord? or who has been His counsellor?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Or who has first given to Him, that it should be recompensed to Him again?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Because of Him, and through Him, and in Him, are all things: to Him be glory for ages of ages.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Let them not, then, dare to pry into that unsearchable question who defend merit before grace, and therefore even against grace, and wish first to give unto God, that it may be given to them again,—first, of course, to give something of free will, that grace may be given them again as a reward; and let them wisely understand or faithfully believe that even what they think that they have first given, they have received from Him, from whom are all things, by whom are all things, in whom are all things.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But why this man should receive, and that should not receive, when neither of them deserves to receive, and whichever of them receives, receives undeservingly,—let them measure their own strength, and not search into things too strong for them.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Let it suffice them to know that there is no unrighteousness with God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For when the apostle could find no merits for which Jacob should take precedence of his twin-brother with God, he said, “What, then, shall we say?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For He says to Moses, I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy, and I will show compassion on whom I will show compassion.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore it is not of him that willeth, nor of him that runneth, but of God that showeth mercy.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Let, therefore, His free compassion be grateful to us, even although this profound question be still unsolved; which, nevertheless, is so far solved as the same apostle solves it, saying, “But if God, willing to show His wrath, and to demonstrate His power, endured in much patience the vessels of wrath which are fitted to destruction; and that He might make known the riches of His glory on the vessels of mercy, which He has prepared for glory.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Certainly wrath is not repaid unless it is due, lest there be unrighteousness with God; but mercy, even when it is bestowed, and not due, is not unrighteousness with God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And hence, let the vessels of mercy understand how freely mercy is afforded to them, because to the vessels of wrath with whom they have common cause and measure of perdition, is repaid wrath, righteous and due.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: This is now enough in opposition to those who, by freedom of will, desire to destroy the liberality of grace.
